item.title: Intellectual Property Protection
item.description: How patent systems, trade secret law, and copyright interact with market structure, with a focus on optimal IP policy design in open economies.

item.title: International Trade
item.description: The role of institutional quality — particularly IP protection — as a determinant of bilateral trade patterns, using gravity models with legal and institutional variables.

item.title: Law & Economics
item.description: Applying mechanism design and contract theory to IP law: patent breadth, licensing terms, and the interaction between competition law and intellectual property.

item.title: On the optimal breadth of patents in developing economies
item.description: Why broad patents may create stronger disincentives for technology adoption in countries with weaker enforcement capacity, even when they nominally comply with TRIPS.

item.title: Gravity models and the zeroes problem
item.description: Revisiting the Poisson pseudo-maximum likelihood estimator for trade flows with many zero observations — practical notes on Stata and R implementation.

item.title: Trade Agreements and IP Harmonization: Evidence from TRIPS
item.description: Presentation at the Annual Conference on International Economics (2025).

item.title: Technology Transfer under Heterogeneous Intellectual Property Regimes
item.description: This paper develops a framework to analyze how heterogeneous IP regimes affect international technology transfer and innovation. Using a gravity model with PPML estimation, I exploit TRIPS accession as a quasi-natural experiment to identify the causal effect of IP harmonization on bilateral technology flows.

item.title: Cross-Border Licensing and Market Power in Developing Countries
item.description: Ongoing research project examining licensing dynamics and market power in developing economies.

item.title: Optimal Patent Length in Open Economies: A Law and Economics Approach
item.description: This paper examines optimal patent duration in open economies with heterogeneous enforcement capacity.

item.title: Intellectual Property Rights in the Global Economy
item.description: The foundational empirical reference on how IP rights affect FDI, licensing, and technology transfer across income groups.

item.title: International Protection of Intellectual Property
item.description: Optimal patent policy in an integrated world economy. Frequently cited in current work on patent length.

item.title: Technology Transfer
item.description:
Technology transfer — the diffusion of knowledge, innovation, and productive capacity across firms and national borders — is one of the central mechanisms through which global economic integration translates into productivity growth and convergence. My research in this area examines how the institutional environment, particularly intellectual property protection regimes, shapes both the incentives to transfer technology and the channels through which diffusion occurs.

Core Questions

The fundamental tension in technology transfer policy is between the static and dynamic effects of intellectual property protection. Strong IP rights may encourage knowledge disclosure and formal licensing, but they may also raise the cost of technology access for firms in developing countries. Understanding how this trade-off plays out across different institutional environments — and identifying the conditions under which IP harmonization promotes rather than restricts diffusion — is the central motivation for my empirical research.

item.title: The Colonial Origins of Comparative Development: An Empirical Investigation
item.description: Acemoglu, Johnson, and Robinson (2001) provide the foundational empirical framework for understanding how institutions — rather than geography or culture alone — drive long-run economic development. Using settler mortality as an instrument for institutional quality, they demonstrate that property rights protection has a large causal effect on per capita income.
